title: Cost Accounting & Managerial Basics
subhead: The accounting that runs the inside of the business — job costing, variance analysis, and cost centers. Getting this right turns a single P&L into a project-by-project profitability map.
hook: "Is this project actually making money?" Every services firm with multiple jobs asks it monthly, and a single blended gross margin hides the answer. Job costing reveals it. Variance analysis explains why it moved. Both get you promoted to senior.

chunks.title: Job Costing vs. Process Costing — Which One Applies to You
chunks.bodyHtml:
Every services firm and manufacturer tracks costs differently. Getting the model wrong makes your P&L by project useless.

Job Costing: costs tracked per specific job/project. Every cost (labor, materials, overhead) is assigned to a specific job number. This is Skarion's model — every fiber-design project has its own job code (Tennessee Corridor, Texas Long-Haul, Virginia Metro).

Process Costing: costs averaged across a continuous production process (paint factory, oil refinery, bottling line). Not relevant to Skarion, but you must recognize it — it's what a manufacturer's books look like and what gets tested in interviews.

Direct vs. Indirect costs — the distinction that drives everything:

  • Direct: easily traced to a specific job — offshore drafting hours on Project Alpha, dedicated software licenses for a project.
  • Indirect / Overhead: cannot be directly traced — office rent, utilities, management salaries. These must be allocated across jobs by some fair basis (revenue %, headcount %, hours %).

chunks.title: Skarion Job Costing Lab — Gross Profit by Project
chunks.bodyHtml:
Three active Skarion projects. Allocate costs correctly to each and reveal gross profit per project.

ProjectContract valueDirect offshore labor (at $38.50/mi × billed miles)Direct software (allocated by project hours)Indirect overhead (rent + utilities, allocated by revenue %)Total costGross profitGM %
Tennessee Corridor$1,200,000$462,000$72,000$20,000$554,000$646,00053.8%
Texas Long-Haul$800,000$327,250$48,000$13,333$388,583$411,41751.4%
Virginia Metro$350,000$308,000$21,000$5,833$334,833$15,1674.3%

callout.html: The key insight. Skarion's blended 70.8% gross margin hides that Tennessee Corridor is making 53.8% (on contract value) while Virginia Metro is barely break-even at 4.3% — its offshore labor cost swallowed almost the entire contract. Job costing reveals which projects to renew, renegotiate, or sunset. A single blended number never tells you that.

chunks.title: Variance Analysis — What Went Wrong and Where
chunks.bodyHtml:
Variance analysis explains why actual results differ from budget. It's the skill that gets accountants promoted to senior faster than anything else.

The vocabulary:

  • Budget vs. Actual — every month differs; variance analysis explains why.
  • Favorable variance (F) — actual is better than budget (lower cost or higher revenue).
  • Unfavorable variance (U) — actual is worse than budget.
  • Price variance — the cost per unit was different from budget.
  • Volume/Efficiency variance — you used more or fewer units than budgeted.

Skarion January example — decompose the total variance into its two causes:

  • Budgeted offshore drafting: $38.00/mi × 3,200 mi = $121,600
  • Actual offshore drafting: $41.50/mi × 3,380 mi = $140,270
  • Total variance: $18,670 Unfavorable
  • Price variance: ($41.50 − $38.00) × 3,380 mi = $11,830 U (paid more per mile than budgeted)
  • Volume variance: (3,380 − 3,200) × $38.00 = $6,840 U (used more miles than budgeted)

Splitting the variance tells the PM two different stories: the price variance means renegotiate the rate; the volume variance means the scope estimate was wrong. Different causes, different fixes.

chunks.title: Variance Vocabulary — 5 Cards with Skarion Examples

cards.back: Total Actual − Total Budget. Skarion January offshore drafting: $140,270 actual − $121,600 budget = $18,670 Unfavorable. The top-line gap before you decompose it.
(Actual price − Budget price) × Actual volume. Skarion: ($41.50 − $38.00) × 3,380 = $11,830 U. Tells you the rate was wrong — renegotiate the MSA or the PO.
(Actual volume − Budget volume) × Budget price. Skarion: (3,380 − 3,200) × $38.00 = $6,840 U. Tells you the scope/estimate was wrong — fix the planning, not the rate.

Actual is better than budget — lower cost or higher revenue. Skarion example: if actual miles came in at 3,000 vs 3,200 budgeted, the volume variance is $7,600 Favorable (you used fewer miles than planned).
Actual is worse than budget — higher cost or lower revenue. Skarion's January offshore drafting was $18,670 U, split into $11,830 U price + $6,840 U volume. Always decompose before you act.

chunks.title: Cost Centers, Profit Centers, and How Big Companies Think
chunks.bodyHtml:
Once you move beyond a 10-person company, accounting is organized by segment. Understanding this gets you hired at larger firms.

Segment typeDefinitionExample
Cost CenterIncurs costs but doesn't directly generate revenueIT, HR, Legal — and the accounting team itself
Profit CenterGenerates its own revenue and has its own P&LEach regional office, each product line
Investment CenterA profit center that also controls its own capital investment decisionsA division that approves its own capex

How this shows up: cost-center accounting requires allocating shared costs (rent, utilities, management overhead) across departments. Three common allocation bases — by headcount %, by square footage, or by revenue %. Each gives a different answer; the "right" method depends on what drives the cost (rent → square footage; IT support → headcount).

Intercompany transactions: when a cost center in one entity charges another entity, it creates an intercompany receivable/payable that must be eliminated in consolidation — otherwise the group's total revenue/expenses are inflated by what one part charged another.

Skarion context: if Skarion expands to two offices (Virginia = HLD, Texas = LLD), each is a profit center; the IT team supporting both is a cost center; the controller must allocate IT costs to both offices fairly — and eliminate the internal charges when consolidating.

chunks.title: Allocation Methods Quiz — Which Is Most Equitable?
chunks.intro: Skarion has $8,000/month of IT and office overhead to allocate across three cost centers (Engineering 12 staff, Sales 4 staff, Accounting 2 staff). Pick the method and defend it.

questions.q: Allocating the $8,000 IT overhead by headcount — Engineering pays how much?
questions.explanation: Headcount allocation: 12/18 = 66.7% × $8,000 = $5,333... actually $8,000 × 12/18 = $5,333. Use revenue % if IT tracks projects (which it does at Skarion).

questions.q: If the IT team tracks its time per project, which allocation base is MOST equitable for IT cost?
options: Actual project hours consumed (a usage/driver base)
questions.explanation: When a usage driver (project hours) is measurable, allocate by it — it ties cost to the actual consumption, not an arbitrary proxy. This is activity-based costing at its simplest.

questions.q: Two subsidiaries share a $50K cost center. Sub A charges Sub B $30K for services. At consolidation, what happens to the $30K?
options: It's eliminated entirely (offset against B's payable to A)
It's recorded twice — once per entity
It's written off as bad debt
questions.explanation: Intercompany elimination: the $30K receivable on A's books offsets the $30K payable on B's, and the revenue/expense from the charge cancels out. Consolidated numbers must reflect only external transactions.
